ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for mango sticky rice as a composite dish; 'sticky rice food product' is the closest defensible parent class. Mango and coconut cream components are not individually coded here.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice-based dessert — glutinous rice with fruit and coconut cream
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Thai mango sticky rice. A0CKP (rice-based dessert) is the closest defensible category anchor. Composite dish with mango and coconut cream components.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for mango sticky rice or Thai glutinous rice dessert as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard dessert serving of mango sticky rice is approximately 300 g, comprising roughly 150 g of coconut-infused glutinous rice, 120 g of fresh mango slices, and 30 g of coconut cream drizzle.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Glutinous Rice (cooked), Mango (raw), Coconut Cream — composite estimation; cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Nutrient Composition of Food database for coconut-based dessert analogues
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Thai mango sticky rice as a composite dish. Nutrient values were estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: cooked glutinous rice (FDC ID 168872), raw mango (FDC ID 169910), and coconut cream (FDC ID 170172), weighted at approximately 50% glutinous rice, 40% mango, and 10% coconut cream by mass. The input record's per_100g values (energy 150 kcal, protein 1.83 g, fat 6.0 g, carbohydrate 22.67 g, sugar 11.67 g, sodium 50 mg, dietary fibre 1.0 g) are consistent with this component-weighted estimate and are retained. Singapore HPB database consulted for coconut-based dessert analogues to cross-validate saturated fat and sodium values.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — glutinous rice (sticky rice) analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists in the University of Sydney GI Database specifically for Thai mango sticky rice as a composite dish. GI of 86 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate component: cooked glutinous (waxy) rice, which is reported at GI 86 (glucose reference) in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and corroborated by the University of Sydney GI Database entry for glutinous rice. Mango contributes additional sugar but the glutinous rice dominates the glycaemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (86 × 68.0) / 100 = 58.6, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 22.67 × 3 = 68.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (11.67 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.0 g/100 g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium itself is low at 50 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Although sodium is low at 50 mg/100 g, the high sugar load (11.67 g/100 g) with minimal fibre (1.0 g/100 g) triggers insulin-mediated sodium retention and should be limited to occasional small portions in individuals with hypertension.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic index (GI 86) from glutinous rice combined with 11.67 g sugar/100 g and only 1.0 g dietary fibre/100 g causes rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mango sticky rice is high-GI (GI 86) with a glycemic load of 58.6 per standard 300 g serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit portion size, and if consumed, pair with a protein or high-fibre food to partially attenuate the glucose spike.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk at 5.0 g/100 g (medium-chain saturated fatty acids) and high sugar content (11.67 g/100 g) contribute to atherogenic and metabolic risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content of 5.0 g/100 g from coconut cream, combined with 11.67 g sugar/100 g, raises LDL-cholesterol and triglyceride risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional small servings and prioritise lower-saturated-fat dessert alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat at 5.0 g/100 g from coconut milk and sugar at 11.67 g/100 g may promote pro-inflammatory cytokine activity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Coconut milk is high in saturated fat which can promote inflammatory cytokines; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit frequency of consumption and request smaller portions to reduce the pro-inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mango Sticky Rice (Thai: ข้าวเหนียวมะม่วง, romanized: Khao Niao Mamuang) is a classic Thai dessert consisting of sweet glutinous (waxy) rice cooked by steaming and infused with sweetened coconut milk, served alongside slices of ripe, fragrant mango and drizzled with rich coconut cream. The glutinous rice has a soft, chewy, slightly sticky texture that absorbs the coconut milk during cooking, resulting in a mildly sweet, creamy, and subtly salty rice base. The fresh mango provides a juicy, fibrous contrast with natural sweetness and a hint of tartness. The coconut cream drizzle adds richness and a characteristic tropical aroma. This dish is widely consumed across Thailand and Southeast Asia, particularly during mango season (April–June), and is popular in Singapore as a Thai dessert offering. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 150 kcal, 1.83 g protein, 6.0 g fat (of which 5.0 g is saturated fat from coconut), and 22.67 g carbohydrate with 11.67 g sugar and only 1.0 g dietary fibre. The glycemic index is high (GI 86), driven by the glutinous rice, making this dish a significant concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or insulin resistance. The saturated fat from coconut milk is relevant for cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis management. Vitamin C from mango contributes approximately 10 mg/100 g. The dish is naturally gluten-free, vegan, vegetarian, and halal. A standard dessert serving is approximately 300 g, delivering a glycemic load of approximately 58.6.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Thai mango sticky rice as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked glutinous rice, raw mango, and coconut cream, weighted by approximate mass proportions. The input record values are consistent with this component-weighted estimate and have been retained. GI is assigned by analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for glutinous rice (GI 86). Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact dish-level approved-source match was found.
notes: 1. Nutrient values estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (cooked glutinous rice FDC 168872, raw mango FDC 169910, coconut cream FDC 170172) weighted at approximately 50% glutinous rice, 40% mango, 10% coconut cream by mass. Input record values are consistent with this estimate and retained. Singapore HPB database consulted for cross-validation of coconut-based dessert analogues. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this composite Thai dessert preparation are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026; component-level data exist but composite dish-level values are not published. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — coconut milk and mango contain no dietary cholesterol; this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived cholesterol-containing ingredients. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.3 g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.1 g) estimated from USDA FoodData Central coconut cream and mango component fatty acid profiles; these are approximate. 5. GI of 86 is an analogous estimate based on glutinous rice GI from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) and University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for the composite dish was found in approved sources. 6. FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are approximate matches; no exact ontology code exists for Thai mango sticky rice as a composite dish. 7. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was not used as a primary nutrient source as it does not qualify as an approved primary source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. 8. Input cr_id G00012397 and menu name 'D1 Mango Tango Classic' preserved in aliases.
